# **Research Question: How much racial information about police-killing victims is carried by "race-blind" features, and how close does a race-blind prediction come to a benchmark that uses the tract's racial composition directly?**

## **The question and why it is interesting**

Public discussions of algorithmic fairness in policing often focus on whether predictive systems explicitly use race as an input. A model that excludes race as a feature is sometimes described as "race-blind." This framing assumes that non-racial features carry no racial information, but in practice, features such as neighborhood poverty, unemployment, and education are correlated with race through U.S. residential segregation and socioeconomic history. This project tests that assumption directly. The core question is whether we can predict a victim's race (White, Black, or Hispanic/Latino) using only features that do not explicitly reference the racial makeup of the surrounding neighborhood. To put the answer in context, we also fit a full-information benchmark that adds the tract's explicit racial composition. The benchmark is not a competing prediction task, because a model that knows a victim was killed in a 90 percent Black tract can trivially guess the victim is Black. Instead, it serves as an **upper bound** that quantifies how much of the total available racial signal a race-blind predictor can recover. The analysis is restricted to the three racial groups large enough to model reliably (White *n* \= 235, Black *n* \= 133, Hispanic/Latino *n* \= 66\) and uses 434 victims with complete covariates. Victims coded as "Unknown," "Asian/Pacific Islander," or "Native American" are excluded because there are too few observations in those categories for a three-way or broader classifier.

## **Methodology**

We constructed a ladder of four nested feature sets and compared three modeling algorithms on each. Feature set A contains only incident-level variables: victim age, gender, cause of death, and whether the victim was armed (collapsed into three categories: Firearm, Other weapon, or Unarmed). Feature set B contains only tract-level socioeconomic features: population, median household income, poverty rate, unemployment rate, and the share of adults with a college degree. Feature set C combines A and B, which is our primary race-blind predictor, representing what a classifier built on incident and neighborhood features could achieve. Feature set D is our upper-bound benchmark and adds the three tract racial-composition variables on top of C: the share of the tract population that is non-Hispanic White (*share\_white*), Black alone (*share\_black*), and Hispanic/Latino of any race (*share\_hispanic*). These three variables directly report the tract's racial makeup, which is why set D is treated as a ceiling rather than a competing prediction. On each feature set we fit three models. A multinomial logistic regression is a classical statistical model for categorical outcomes with three or more classes that estimates the log-odds of each class as a weighted sum of the predictors. A random forest is an ensemble of 500 decision trees trained on random subsamples whose votes are averaged to produce a prediction and which automatically captures nonlinear feature interactions. A gradient boosting classifier is a second tree-ensemble method that builds trees sequentially so that each new tree corrects the errors of the previous ones. It is included as a sensitivity check to confirm our results do not depend on one specific algorithm. Performance was evaluated using five-fold cross-validation, a procedure in which the data are split into five chunks so every observation receives a prediction from a model that never saw it during training. We report two metrics: overall accuracy (the fraction of victims correctly classified, comparable to the majority-class baseline of 54.1 percent) and the macro-averaged **Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUC)**. An AUC of 0.5 indicates chance performance and 1.0 indicates perfect classification. To test whether the gap between Model C and the Model D benchmark was statistically significant, we used a **paired bootstrap** procedure with 2,000 resamples of the out-of-fold predictions.

## **Results:**

## **The race-blind classifier and how close it gets to the benchmark**

Figure 1 displays classification accuracy (panel a) and macro-averaged AUC (panel b) for all combinations of feature set and model, with the full numeric results in Table 1\. The key comparisons are between feature sets A, B, and C (the race-blind predictors) and feature set D (the full-information benchmark). Before interpreting the model results, we establish the majority-class baseline: the accuracy a trivial classifier would achieve by ignoring every feature and always predicting the most common race in the data (White, 235 of 434 victims, or 54.1 percent). Any useful model must clear this bar, and the distance each model's accuracy sits above it measures how much the features actually help. Using incident features alone (set A), logistic regression barely beats this baseline (55.3 percent accuracy, AUC 0.64), indicating that the circumstances of a killing carry only weak racial signal on their own. Tract socioeconomic features alone (set B) perform comparably (58.5 percent accuracy, AUC 0.65). Combining them in the race-blind classifier (set C) yields 59.5 percent accuracy and AUC 0.68, which is a meaningful and statistically significant improvement over chance, achieved entirely without any variable that reports the tract's racial makeup. The upper-bound benchmark in set D reaches 72.4 percent accuracy and AUC 0.83, and the paired bootstrap test confirms that the gap between C and D is real (mean accuracy difference of 12.9 percentage points, 95% confidence interval \[8.7, 17.3\], p \< 0.001). Translating these numbers into a single headline: on the AUC scale from chance (0.5) to ceiling (0.83), our race-blind classifier captures (0.68 − 0.50) / (0.83 − 0.50) ≈ 55 percent of the available racial signal without ever using a variable that directly reports neighborhood race. The three modeling algorithms produce similar rankings across feature sets, with logistic regression and random forest nearly tied and gradient boosting slightly weaker; this close agreement indicates the finding is robust and not an artifact of any single algorithm's assumptions.

![][image1]

| Feature set | LR accuracy | LR AUC | RF accuracy | RF AUC | GB accuracy | GB AUC |
| :---- | ----: | ----: | ----: | ----: | ----: | ----: |
| \[Majority-class baseline\] | 0.541 | — | — | — | — | — |
| A. Incident only | 0.553 | 0.640 | 0.532 | 0.629 | 0.502 | 0.584 |
| B. Tract SES only | 0.585 | 0.647 | 0.574 | 0.648 | 0.551 | 0.594 |
| **C. Incident \+ SES (race-blind)** | **0.595** | **0.678** | **0.588** | **0.704** | **0.555** | **0.639** |
| *D. Upper-bound benchmark (C \+ tract race)* | *0.724* | *0.830* | *0.703* | *0.825* | *0.666* | *0.796* |

*Table 1\. Cross-validated accuracy and macro-averaged AUC across the four feature sets and three modeling algorithms. **LR** stands for multinomial logistic regression, **RF** for random forest, and **GB** for gradient boosting. **Accuracy** is the proportion of victims correctly classified (directly comparable to the majority-class baseline of 0.541). **AUC** is the macro-averaged one-versus-rest Area Under the ROC Curve, where 0.5 represents chance performance and 1.0 represents perfect classification. Row C is the primary race-blind predictor; row D is the upper-bound benchmark that includes the tract's explicit racial composition.*

## **Where the race-blind classifier succeeds and where it fails**

Figure 2 decomposes AUC by racial group to show which classes are easier to predict with race-blind features. Under set C, per-class AUCs are remarkably similar across the three groups, Black 0.67, Hispanic 0.67, White 0.69, meaning no single race is dramatically easier or harder to predict from neutral features alone. 

However, Figure 3 reveals an important asymmetry that the AUC numbers alone hide. Under the race-blind Model C (Figure 3a), the classifier correctly labels 85 percent of White victims but only 41 percent of Black victims, and a striking 5 percent of Hispanic victims; it predicts "Hispanic" for only 11 of the 434 cases in the entire sample. In other words, the race-blind model essentially collapses into a Black-versus-White classifier and treats Hispanic victims as a residual category almost never actively predicted. The upper-bound benchmark in Figure 3b confirms that this failure is not inherent to the three-class problem: with tract racial composition available, the benchmark correctly identifies 44 percent of Hispanic victims and 59 percent of Black victims. The distinction matters because it shows that race-blind features are particularly poor at identifying Hispanic victims specifically, the tract's socioeconomic profile alone does not distinguish Hispanic neighborhoods from White neighborhoods as sharply as it distinguishes Black ones. This is a concrete failure mode with real-world consequences: any downstream decision system relying on a race-blind classifier would systematically mis-categorize Hispanic victims, even if overall accuracy looks acceptable.  
![][image2]

## **What drives the race-blind predictions**

Figure 4 shows which features matter most. Panel 4a reports permutation importance for the random forest on the upper-bound benchmark (Model D), which measures how much accuracy drops when each feature's values are randomly shuffled across the dataset. The three tract racial-composition variables dominate the ranking, each dropping accuracy by roughly 8 percentage points when permuted. This ordering confirms why we treat D as a benchmark rather than a competing prediction: when those variables are available, the model leans on them heavily. Panel 4b focuses on the race-blind Model C and shows the standardized multinomial logistic-regression coefficients for the six continuous non-race features, separated by predicted class. The patterns are consistent and interpretable. Older victim age strongly predicts White classification (coefficient \+0.48) and lower Black or Hispanic probability. High tract unemployment and high tract college attainment push predictions toward Black (coefficients \+0.38 and \+0.30). High tract poverty and high tract household income push predictions toward Hispanic (+0.26 and \+0.17). These coefficients reflect real structural patterns in the U.S. population: for example, Black-majority tracts in this sample tend to have *both* higher unemployment *and* a larger share of college-educated residents than the typical tract, while Hispanic neighborhoods tend to have higher poverty. These are precisely the kinds of proxy relationships that make "race-blind" classifiers not truly race-blind in practice.  
![][image3]

## Answer and interpretation

The race-blind classifier, which is a model trained only on incident features and tract socioeconomic variables, with no direct information about the racial makeup of the neighborhood, predicts the race of police-killing victims with accuracy well above the majority-class baseline (59.5 percent versus 54.1 percent) and with AUC 0.68 against a chance baseline of 0.50. Comparing this to the upper-bound benchmark that includes tract racial composition (AUC 0.83), the race-blind classifier recovers roughly 55 percent of the total available racial signal without ever seeing a variable that explicitly reports neighborhood race. The confusion-matrix analysis sharpens this conclusion by showing where the race-blind model has the following weakness: it is able to predict Black vs White victims and fails to actively predict Hispanic victims. In a country with substantial residential segregation, features such as age, poverty, unemployment, and education carry a meaningful amount of racial information. The main limitations are that the dataset covers only the first half of 2015 and only fatal encounters, the three racial groups we analyze are the only ones with enough observations to model, and the definitions of the three share variables in the data are not mutually exclusive (*share\_white* is non-Hispanic white, *share\_black* is Black alone, and *share\_hispanic* is Hispanic of any race), so they are best interpreted as standard Census measures of racial composition rather than a clean partition of the tract population.
